sentences.text: My name is Gerald-- G-E-R-A-L-D-- Steinberg-- S-T-E-I-N-B-E-R-G.
Zelik-- Z-E-L-I-K-- and the same last name, Steinberg.
December 8, 1939.
I am 57.
I was born Mlynov-- M-L-Y-N-O-V-- Poland.
In a house located on a farm.
Paula Steinberg.
Her maiden name was Wurtzel, W-U-R-T-Z-E-L.
George Steinberg.
Correct.
It was in the countryside of the Mlynov.
It was near the Russian border.
I guess it's eastern Poland.
So later on known as the Ukraine.
The largest city was Dubno, D-U-B-N-O.
I'm told maybe 10 or 15 kilometers.
I guess they had a little bit of everything.
Cows and horses, and also grew some hay, and wheat, and so forth.
No.
Well, prior to the war, of course, my father, since he got married, moved into his own house.
Of course, prior to that our whole family lived in the general area of Mlynov and another little small town called Muravic.
Well, I--
Well, we stayed there up until the time that the Germans came in, which was approximately, maybe, I was about two years old when they came.
I really don't remember personally, obviously, since I was only two years old.
But I'm told that when the Germans came in, they put all the Jewish residents of the towns of Mlynov and Muravic into a ghetto.
And, of course, the ghetto was surrounded by wire and so forth.
And the Jews were contained inside.
And I'm told they would take certain ones to do work outside the ghetto at times, and-- and everything else that went along with being incarcerated in a ghetto.
My parents, and my aunt and uncle.
And also, I had a cousin who also survived.
He's about two years older than I am.
And he remembered a few things, as well.
And every time our families would get together, certain things would be brought up, and discussed.
It was, obviously, confiscated by the Germans when they came in, and then eventually just became part of the Russian property when the Russians liberated us and we left the area.
Well, briefly my parents and I returned to the farm after the war when we were liberated by the Russians, but we were just there for a very short period of time.
Well, the ghetto was a combined Jewish population of Mlynov and Muravic.
So it was in-- in that general area.
There were just a few thousand Jewish inhabitants from both towns.
There were very few people there.
In the ghetto was just my father, my mother, and I.
But there were other-- obviously, other members of my mother's family and my father's family were also in the ghetto as well.
Yes.
My mother and my father.
We were in the ghetto approximately about a year.
I don't remember anything specifically.

I am told of an incident where my parents have told me that when the word got out that the Germans came in, I-- I hid-- hid out, and they were looking for me everywhere, and I had hidden myself somewhere.
In the house.
I don't know.
I guess it was just a fear or whatever, and I suppose listening to what was going on, and so on.
But I personally don't remember any specifics during that time.
Well, when the Germans came into our area, I'm told, of course, they rounded up the Jews and marched them to the ghetto.
They also forced some of them to do certain labor.

And I believe they usually kind of tried to shame the religious people that had-- like rabbis who had beards, they would shave their beards, and the long sideburns that religious Jews wear, they would cut those down, I'm told.
Just to show them, humiliate them, in front of their-- their flock.
I believe it was 1942.

About a year after we got in, my father had somehow gotten some information about some digging that was going on outside the ghetto that the Germans claimed were to be used for storing potatoes for the winter.

However, somehow, some information that my father had gathered, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as it turned out later on, it was true that it turned out to be the grave, the mass grave for the people in the ghetto.
But my father was able to smuggle my mother and myself out just a few days prior to when they killed everyone in the ghetto.
Most of my mother's family and most of my father's family were all killed during that time.
And some of them also escaped from the ghetto, but were later caught and also killed.
Most of our family died from both sides in-- in the ghetto.
None from my mother's family.
She was the only survivor.
However, my father did have one sister and one brother survive.
And in total, there were seven brothers and sisters in my father's family.
And of course, three survived and four died.
Also, my father's mother, my grandmother, was also killed during that time.
Mostly through my father and my mother telling me this.
And of course, the people that survived I knew and lived with throughout the years later.
Correct.
First, my father somehow found a way to smuggle my mother and I out of the ghetto first.
And we were picked up by a gentile friend of my father's outside the ghetto who gave us a ride to his farm.
And later on, my father joined us.
And we hid out for about a month or two at this gentile's farm in a-- in a hay stack in the-- in the-- in the barn.
And unfortunately, we were unable to stay there for any longer, because this farmer was on some type of committee or whatever with the Germans.
He did work for them.
And there were a lot of people coming and going.
And it was not safe for him to keep us, of course.
So we had to leave.
[NON-ENGLISH], I believe was his name.
That was the last name.
I don't remember the first name.
But this farmer, who was a good friend of my father's, helped us out at the beginning.
I don't think it was prearranged other than a short period of time.
My father became very suspicious, and he said, now's the time to go.
And so--
I think it was more of a coincidence that the farmer was there to give us a ride.
Because originally, I'm told, my mother and I were just walking along.
Of course, the means of transportation was just either a horse and buggy or walking in the area.
I think there was a gate, but my father was on a committee called the Judenrat.
It was kind of a committee comprised of Jews that were like the go-betweens, the population and the German overseers.
And as a result, there were certain things that my father was privy to, more so than the others, of course.
And he also had certain privileges.
And somehow he was able to get my mother and myself out of the ghetto through the gate.

Of course, the minute that we're out of the gate, I'm told, we took off the Jewish stars that all the Jews were asked to-- to wear, were forced to wear, so we wouldn't be identified as being Jews immediately.
I'm told this is what happened.
My mother and I escaped in daylight.
My father later on.
I believe it was already after dark that he was able to get out.
The same day, yes.
It was just a very short period of time.
Maybe one or two months.
Maybe a little longer.
Again, he was very nervous about having us there in hiding.
And so we had left.
We were forced to leave and find another place to stay.

When the farmer became nervous about us staying and essentially asked us to leave, my father persuaded him to keep us there for a short period of time while he went out and contacted other gentile friends, farmers, fellow farmers, with whom he had dealt over the years.
And he was able to persuade another farmer to take us in.
And then, my father came back and picked my mother and I up.
And we went to hide in-- in this new location.
And at that time, my father also found out the whereabouts of his sister.
And he also went and got her and brought her with us.
So we'd be-- the four of us started hiding out at that point.
Her first time was Bunya, B-U-N-Y-A.
Steinberg, of course, at that time.
While my father was looking for places, we were still in the-- in the barn in a haystack, hiding out.
We were hiding in a stack of hay, essentially, in a barn.
I think it was just the barn was full of hay.
They would store it.
And of course, the barns in those days and in that area were not the same as we know it now.
They were just like-- the roofs were made out of hay themselves.
And-- and some of them were very simple structures.
No, no.
We would stay there the whole time, of course, for fear of being discovered.
If we were discovered, obviously, we would all be killed, as well as the people that were hiding us were also in danger of being killed, as was happened many times to others.
Food was almost non-existent.
Even the farmers themselves had, you know, very meager food availability.
We would actually sometimes share food that they would throw out to the pigs and so forth.
My father would, you know, go out, and kind of scavenge through and find things.
And we would eat-- occasionally, he'd go out and find berries and things in the woods, and so forth.
And somehow we were able to maintain staying alive.
Yes.
Yes.
From this first farmer who kept us for two or three months, my father found another farmer that was willing to take us in.
And at this time, he dug a hole in a barn.
The farmer and my father.
They dug a hole in the farm inside where they kept the cows in a barn.
And they covered up with planks, and of course, with straw over it.
And they made an entrance through the manger trough where they fed the cows.
And that was the entrance to go in inside the hole.
The hole was quite small, just about held the four of us.
Certainly you couldn't stand up.
Or if one person turned, I'm told, everybody else had to turn because there was so little room.
And this is the way we hid out for the whole time.
Not the whole time, but we had two other locations after that.
I remember just little things about during the time that we were hiding out.
Occasionally, I would go up to the attic of the barn.
And I remember going up there and seeing a little bit of daylight, because, of course, it was very dark constantly in the-- in the hole.
And that used to be a real treat, getting up there.
And in those days, the farmers used to dry fruits and things in the attic.
And occasionally, I'd find some little things to eat there.

And there was also a time-- this was not very pleasant-- which I remember-- and I'm told I was very good at this-- but I remember, it was-- we had-- what little clothes we had were infested with lice, and that was a time, of course, that I was able to see.
And I was very good in finding the lice in-- in the seams, and kind of killing them between my nails.
That part I remember personally, and so on.
And that used to happen on rare occasions, where I would be able to go out into the attic and see daylight.
It was just always very dark, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very cramped, and wet constantly, very moist and so forth.
It was just lined with straw and so on.
And I just remember I had to be very quiet, of course.

And I'm told that at times, being a young child, and for some reason I would be uncomfortable, and I would cry, my mother would have to kind of muffle my sound to a point where she would almost choke me.
And I'm also told that I learned to cry mimicking the sounds of a cat or a dog animal so that we wouldn't be discovered.
And I also remember my mother telling me stories about how things will be when we were liberated later on, kind of just to keep me amused, if you will.
She would talk about having, you know, good food, and being able to cook again.
And-- and we would be living in a house again in the-- in the-- in the daylight.
Those are some of the things that she used to talk about.
Those were big things in those days.
I don't recall anyone specifically being sick.
I know my mother, I guess, had from the dampness rheumatism arthritis.
And that would bother her a great deal.
And at one time, when we had to escape out of the hole because the barn was set afire, she was so badly inflicted that she wasn't able to lift herself out of the hole.
And she actually stayed there with my-- my aunt after my father and I escaped out of the hole.
Yeah.
At the-- at the first farm that we stayed in in the hole, we stayed there for maybe eight or nine months.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the barn was set afire.
And my-- my mother finally persuaded my father to-- she was having difficulty getting out-- to take me and-- and run out of the barn.
And my father grabbed me and ran out into the wheat fields.
And it was late summer then, so the-- the-- the wheat was quite high.
Or corn, I don't remember exactly what it was.
We were able to hide in there.
And this was during the night.
And my mother and my aunt remained in a hole.
But through some miracle, my mother was able to get herself out of the hole with my aunt, and they, too, escaped out of the barn.
And eventually we met up in the area of the-- of the fields.
And we were reunited.
I do remember personally being out during the night and seeing these balls of fire overhead just going across.
I-- I don't recall.
It might have been shooting, or whatever.
But I remember seeing these balls of fire going overhead.
During that time, even though it was German-occupied, the Polish residents and the Ukrainian residents used to have, like, little civil wars between them.
And I believe the Ukrainian set the barn on the fire during that time, I'm told, or I've overheard my parents discuss it.
The last name was Sharek, S-H-A-R-E-K.
I believe that's the way he would have spelled the name.
And he was a Polish farmer.
Late, late summer.
Possibly early fall.
And we hid out in-- in the fields and in the woods during that time.
And my father, again, was forced to contact various gentile farmer friends of his, people that he knew over the years, to see if they would take us in as well.
And after a period of a few weeks, I guess, just hiding out in the-- in the woods and in the fields, my father was perhaps able to persuade another farmer.
This time, I believe he was of Ukrainian descent.
And he-- he was willing to take us in.
And again, he dug a hole in a barn.
This time, we already had the experience.
And in the same way, we hid out in his barn also for about eight or nine months.
Fine.
